did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a 3-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for the classification of skin lesions into malignant melanoma and benign classes. The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset, converted to TensorFlow Lite, and deployed on an Android smartphone to obtain diagnostic results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a smartphone-based skin cancer detection system using a 3-layer CNN on the HAM10000 dataset. It details specific methodology (preprocessing, segmentation, augmentation), implementation (TensorFlow Lite, Android Studio), and reports new empirical results (85% accuracy, F1-score of 86%) compared to existing works, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Smartphone-based Skin Cancer Detection using Image Processing and Convolutional Neural Network
journal: 2021 12th International Conference on Computing Communication and Networking Technologies (ICCCNT)
brief_summary: This study proposes a smartphone-based skin cancer detection system to distinguish between malignant melanoma and benign lesions using image processing and a 3-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset, which was preprocessed and segmented using OpenCV on Android devices before being converted to a TensorFlow Lite model for deployment. The researchers compared a segmented approach against a non-segmented approach, evaluating performance using various statistical metrics including accuracy, F1-score, Cohen's kappa, and Matthews correlation coefficient. The segmented approach achieved superior results with 85% accuracy, an F1-score of 86%, a Cohen's kappa coefficient of 0.70, and a Matthews correlation coefficient of 0.71. These results outperformed several existing methodologies listed in the comparison table, demonstrating the efficacy of the proposed method for early skin cancer detection on mobile devices.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes used in the binary classification task. The dataset subset contained 1113 malignant images.

One of the two classes used in the binary classification task. The dataset subset contained 1099 benign images. The paper groups various benign conditions into this single class rather than distinguishing specific benign subtypes.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Malignant Melanoma vs. Benign Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper uses a 3-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify skin lesion images into two discrete classes: malignant melanoma and benign. The model outputs a class label (Benign or Malignant) using a sigmoid activation layer, achieving 85% accuracy and an F1-score of 86% on the HAM10000 dataset subset.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The study used a subset of this dataset containing 2212 images, filtered to include only two classes: malignant melanoma and benign lesions.

use.training: Used for training the 3-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model. The dataset was split into 80% for training.
use.testing: Used for testing the model. The dataset was split into 20% for testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the overall performance of the segmented and non-segmented approaches.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistical metrics such as accuracy, F1-score, Cohen's kappa, and Matthews correlation coefficient, and compared qualitatively against existing works.

HAM10000.description: Subset of HAM10000 dataset containing 2212 images (1113 malignant, 1099 benign). Split into 80% training (1769 images) and 20% testing (443 images).

3-CNN (With Segmentation).notes: Best performing approach. Outperformed non-segmented approach and existing works in Table II.

3-CNN (Without Segmentation).notes: Preprocessed but not segmented. Lagged behind the segmented approach.

preprocessing_techniques: Image Adjustment: Enhancement of brightness and contrast levels to improve luminance intensity and pixel frequency differences.
Noise Removal: Application of a Gaussian filter to reduce noise by removing higher frequencies and passing low frequencies.
Closing Operation: Morphological operation combining dilation followed by erosion to remove human hair noise and other artifacts.
Segmentation: Application of Otsu's Thresholding to segment the skin lesion area (foreground) from the background (foreground) using an iterative process to find the optimal threshold.
Resizing: All images were resized to 150 x 150 pixels.

Data Augmentation: Applied during training including rotation (range 40), horizontal flip, height shift (range 0.2), width shift (range 0.2), zoom (range 0.2), shear (range 0.2), and nearest fill mode.
conclusions: The proposed smartphone-based skin cancer detection system, utilizing a 3-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and image processing, achieved an accuracy of 85% and an F1-score of 86% on the HAM10000 dataset subset.

The segmented approach (preprocessing followed by segmentation using Otsu's Thresholding) outperformed the non-segmented approach, achieving higher accuracy (85% vs 83%), F1-score (86% vs 85%), Cohen's kappa (0.70 vs 0.65), and Matthews correlation coefficient (0.71 vs 0.67).

The proposed 3-CNN model with segmentation demonstrated superior performance compared to existing works using VGG-16, MobileNet, and DenseNet architectures on the same dataset, particularly in terms of accuracy and F1-score.

The best-performing model was successfully converted to a TensorFlow Lite (TFLite) format and deployed on an Android smartphone application, demonstrating the feasibility of mobile-based early skin cancer detection.
The system faces limitations related to image noise, varying illumination levels, image distortion, and limited data availability, which affect efficiency and trustworthiness.
Large blobs or noise structures larger than the suspected skin lesion can lead to inappropriate segmentation, representing a technical limitation of the current methodology.

Future work aims to address these limitations by incorporating more data, using an ensemble of classification models (such as DenseNet-121, VGG-16, Inception V3), and adding precautionary and post-detection tips to the smartphone application.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (Malignant Melanoma vs. Benign lesions), grouping all benign conditions into a single class, which limits clinical specificity for other skin cancers or benign subtypes.

The dataset was restricted to a subset of HAM10000 containing only 2,212 images (1,113 malignant, 1,099 benign), which the authors acknowledge as limited data availability affecting efficiency and trustworthiness.
The segmentation method using Otsu's Thresholding is vulnerable to failure when large blobs or noise structures are larger than the suspected skin lesion, leading to inappropriate segmentation.
The system's performance is sensitive to image noise, varying illumination levels, and image distortion, which are cited as significant challenges.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance comparisons with existing works were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, F1-score) without significance testing.

The model was trained on a computer and deployed on an Android smartphone using TensorFlow Lite, but the preprocessing and segmentation steps (OpenCV) were also implemented on the mobile device, which may impact inference speed and battery life in real-world scenarios.

The comparison with existing works (References 6-10) includes some studies that used different datasets or subsets, making direct performance comparisons potentially confounded by dataset distribution differences.
The paper does not report hyperparameters such as optimizer, loss function, number of epochs, or batch size for the 3-layer CNN training.
